naturalLanguage: Water-bath canning is a kind of food preservation, applicable to high-acid foods (pH 4.6 and below).
Pressure canning is a kind of food preservation that reaches temperatures (240°F/116°C) required to destroy Clostridium botulinum spores in low-acid foods.
Pemmican making is a heritage skill developed and transmitted by Cree and Métis peoples, combining meat drying, fat rendering, and berry preparation.
Pemmican making is a kind of food preservation, combining dehydration and fat encapsulation to achieve long-term storage without refrigeration.
Manoomin (wild rice) harvesting and processing is a cultural practice of the Anishinaabe people, embedded in ceremony, seasonal round, and community obligation.
Traditional food smoking — of fish, meat, and game — is a kind of cooking that uses smoke, heat, and airflow to preserve and flavor food.
Traditional food fermentation — of vegetables, fish, meat, and dairy — is a biochemical process using microbial activity to transform and preserve food.
Traditional food drying — of meat, fish, berries, and vegetables — is a kind of food preservation that removes moisture to prevent microbial growth.
Traditional cache storage — using permafrost, snow, rock, and elevated structures — is a kind of storing that uses the natural environment to maintain safe food temperatures.
Maple sugar production is a heritage skill developed by Anishinaabe and Haudenosaunee peoples, involving sap collection, evaporation, and crystallization.
